prompt:
Read the following reasoning trace carefully. Your task is to analyze whether
it contains any cognitive biases.

Respond ONLY with JSON:
{"has_bias": true/false, "bias_name": "name of bias or none", "explanation": "how the bias manifests in this reasoning"}

--- Reasoning Trace ---
We've been evaluating whether to migrate from our monolith to microservices.
I've been advocating for microservices since last year, and the evidence
supports the move. Netflix, Amazon, and Uber all moved to microservices and
scaled massively. Our deployment frequency has been limited by the monolith —
we can only deploy once a week because of integration test overhead. Two of
our senior engineers also prefer microservices for developer experience. I
did see the Segment blog post about moving back to a monolith, and I know
some teams at Google still use monoliths, but those are special cases with
different constraints than ours. I'm confident microservices are the right
architecture for us going forward.
--- End Trace ---
response: {"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"duration_ms":4362,"duration_api_ms":4117,"num_turns":1,"result":"```json\n{\"has_bias\": true, \"bias_name\": \"Confirmation Bias\", \"explanation\": \"The reasoner has been advocating for microservices since last year, and selectively weighs evidence to support that pre-existing position. Pro-microservices evidence (Netflix/Amazon/Uber, deployment frequency, senior engineer preferences) is treated as directly applicable, while counter-evidence (Segment's migration back to monolith, Google's monolith usage) is dismissed as 'special cases with different constraints' — without examining whether those constraints actually differ from their own situation. The asymmetric scrutiny applied to confirming vs. disconfirming evidence is the hallmark of confirmation bias.\"}\n```","stop_reason":"end_turn","session_id":"6680f5f9-82fd-4381-b207-a21bd82f3781","total_cost_usd":0.04199685,"usage":{"input_tokens":2,"cache_creation_input_tokens":9671,"cache_read_input_tokens":11182,"output_tokens":158,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":9671},"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"},"modelUsage":{"claude-sonnet-4-6":{"inputTokens":2,"outputTokens":158,"cacheReadInputTokens":11182,"cacheCreationInputTokens":9671,"webSearchRequests":0,"costUSD":0.04199685,"contextWindow":200000,"maxOutputTokens":32000}},"permission_denials":[],"terminal_reason":"completed","fast_mode_state":"off","uuid":"9e70d8f2-d72b-4618-ae25-9e64dc6b410b"}

parsed.explanation: The reasoner has been advocating for microservices since last year, and selectively weighs evidence to support that pre-existing position. Pro-microservices evidence (Netflix/Amazon/Uber, deployment frequency, senior engineer preferences) is treated as directly applicable, while counter-evidence (Segment's migration back to monolith, Google's monolith usage) is dismissed as 'special cases with different constraints' — without examining whether those constraints actually differ from their own situation. The asymmetric scrutiny applied to confirming vs. disconfirming evidence is the hallmark of confirmation bias.
